revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri''' {{nihongo|住吉造|Sumiyoshi Zukuri}} is one of the [[honden]] construction styles of [[Shinto Architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]]. The oldest example of this type is from the [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The other main style of honden construction is [[Hachiman-zukuri]]. ([[:ja:八幡造]])
There are three characteristics of the style:
* The pillars, [[rafters]], and [[gables]] are painted vermillion, and the [[panelling|wainscot]] is of white chalk * The roof is made of [[cypress]] bark, and the gables are a strong, straight line * The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyuu]] style.
{{stub}}
[[ja:住吉造]]

The oldest example of this type is from the [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The other main style of honden construction is [[Hachiman-zukuri]]. ([[:ja:八幡造]])
There are three characteristics of the style:
* The pillars, [[rafters]], and [[gables]] are painted vermillion, and the [[panelling|wainscot]] is of white chalk * The roof is made of [[cypress]] bark, and the gables are a strong, straight line * The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyuu]] style.

* The pillars, [[rafters]], and [[gables]] are painted vermillion, and the [[panelling|wainscot]] is of white chalk * The roof is made of [[cypress]] bark, and the gables are a strong, straight line * The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyuu]] style.

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造|}} is one of the [[honden]] construction styles of [[Shinto Architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]]. The oldest example of this type is from the [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The other main style of honden construction is [[Hachiman-zukuri]] ([[:ja:八幡造]]).
There are three characteristics of the style:
* The pillars, [[rafters]], and [[gables]] are painted vermillion, and the [[panelling|wainscot]] is of white chalk * The roof is made of [[cypress]] bark, and the gables are a strong, straight line * The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyuu]] style.

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
The building is 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, and has an entrance under one of the [[gables]].<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> Construction is simple, but the pillars are painted in vermilion and the walls in white. The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyū] style.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.
==Gallery==
<gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}
[[ja:住吉造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
The building is 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, and has an entrance under one of the [[gables]].<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>

The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyū] style.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
The building is 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, and has an entrance under one of the [[gables]].<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> Construction is simple, but the pillars are painted in vermilion and the walls in white. The entrance and exit are lined up in the [[sainyū] style.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
The four identical ''honden'' buildings at Sumiyoshi Taisha are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] and the roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.
==Gallery==
<gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}
[[ja:住吉造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikine sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The four identical ''honden'' buildings at Sumiyoshi Taisha are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] and the roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The four identical ''honden'' buildings at Sumiyoshi Taisha are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] and the roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.

==Notes== {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] and the roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> It is surrounded by a plank ''[[tamagaki]]'' fence (玉垣), which is further surrounded by an ''ara-imi'' fence (荒忌垣).
Its interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/> In both cases, as in many others, there is no veranda.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep, have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] and the roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> It is surrounded by a fence called {{nihongo|mizugaki|瑞垣}} , in itsu turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>

As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[gables]] (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called {{nihongo|[[tamagaki|mizugaki]]|瑞垣}} , in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>

[[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|250 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]]{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''|住吉造}} is an ancient [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''honden'' in [[Ōsaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure==
The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called {{nihongo|[[tamagaki|mizugaki]]|瑞垣}}, in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }}
{{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[:en:Gable|Gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>

==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]] [[ja:住吉造]] [[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]] [[zh:住吉造]] {{wikidata link|Q3503635}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[:en:Gable|Gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[:en:Floor plan|Floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]] [[ja:住吉造]] [[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]] [[zh:住吉造]] {{wikidata link|Q3503635}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[:en:Gable|Gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[:en:Floor plan|Floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}
{{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->]] {{Sumiyoshi Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[:en:Gable|Gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[:en:Floor plan|Floor plan]] </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}
{{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->]] {{Sumiyoshi Faith}}

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]] [[ja:住吉造]] [[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]] [[zh:住吉造]] {{wikidata link|Q3503635}} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|national treasure]] on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the [[:en:Gable|Gable]]s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]]''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]].<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' [[:en:Floor plan|Floor plan]] </gallery>

The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3503635]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}
