<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]]
{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched|1=Thatching}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan|1=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>

==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-zukuri]][[ja:住吉造]][[ru:Сумиёси-дзукури]][[zh:住吉造]]
{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched|1=Thatching}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', and ''sumiyoshi-zukuri''.<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan|1=Floor plan}} </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Sumiyoshi Taisha Hunatama Jinja1.jpg|thumb|200 px|Sumiyoshi Taisha's Funatama Jinja]] {{Nihongo|'''''Sumiyoshi-zukuri'''''|住吉造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} which takes its name from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. As in the case of the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== The ''honden'' on the grounds at Sumiyoshi Taisha has been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=national treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} on the grounds that it is the oldest example of this style of architecture. The four identical ''honden'' buildings that compose it are 4 ''ken'' wide and 2 ''ken'' deep and have an entrance under one of the {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}s (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The roof is simple, doesn't curve upwards at the eaves and is decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal).<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> The building is surrounded by a fence called ''mizugaki'' (瑞垣), in its turn surrounded by another called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}}''|玉垣}} (see image in the gallery).<ref name="jasu"/> There is no veranda, and a short stairway leads to the door.
The interior is divided in two sections, one at the front ({{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣}}) and one at the back ({{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣}}) with a single entrance at the front (see floor plan in the gallery).<ref name="jasu">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sumiyoshizukuri.htm Sumiyoshi-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The structure is simple, but brightly colored: supporting pillars are painted in vermilion and walls in white.
This style is supposed to have its origin in old palace architecture<ref name="jasu"/> Another example of this style is Sumiyoshi Jinja, part of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi Sanjin]] complex in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.<ref name="jasu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Sumiyoshi shrine Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' Image:Sumiyoshi Zukuri.jpg|The ''honden's'' {{ill|Floor plan|en|Floor plan|WD=Q18965|lt=Floor plan|1=Floor plan}} </gallery>
